techniques.name: Signal Fire
techniques.subtitle: Three fires in a triangle = universal distress

steps.instruction: Build 3 fires in a triangle, 100 feet apart.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything in a triangle is the international distress signal. Place on high ground, clearings, or near open water for maximum visibility.

steps.instruction: Prepare each fire with dry tinder and kindling, ready to light.
steps.helpDetail: Have them pre-built so you can light quickly when you hear/see aircraft. Keep fire-starting materials dry and ready.

steps.instruction: Add green branches or wet material for heavy smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke is visible from much greater distance than flame. Green leaves, wet grass, or rubber produce thick white/black smoke.

steps.instruction: At night, flames are more visible than smoke.
steps.helpDetail: At night, keep fires burning with dry wood for bright flames. During day, switch to smoky materials.

techniques.name: Signal Mirror
techniques.subtitle: Visible 50+ miles on a clear day

steps.instruction: Any reflective surface works: mirror, CD, phone screen, metal.
steps.helpDetail: Commercial signal mirrors are the best but ANY reflective object can work. Even a belt buckle or aluminum foil.

steps.instruction: Hold the mirror near your eye and extend your other hand toward the target.
steps.helpDetail: The target is the aircraft, boat, or distant point you want to signal.

steps.instruction: Reflect sunlight onto your extended hand, then move hand to align flash with target.
steps.helpDetail: The bright spot on your hand tells you where the reflection is pointing. Move it until it's on the target. Sweep back and forth across the target.

steps.instruction: Flash in sets of three (SOS pattern) — three short, three long, three short.
steps.helpDetail: Keep signaling even if you think the aircraft hasn't seen you. Pilots scan with binoculars and may see you long before you realize it.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Signals
techniques.subtitle: Large visible markers for aircraft

steps.instruction: Create symbols at least 10 feet tall, 3 feet wide.
steps.helpDetail: Bigger is always better. Use contrasting materials against the ground — dark rocks on light sand, light clothing on dark soil.

steps.instruction: Standard signals: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, → = traveling this direction.
steps.helpDetail: I = need medical supplies. F = need food and water. LL = all is well. These are ICAO standard ground-to-air visual signals.

steps.instruction: Place in open areas visible from above — clearings, hilltops, beaches.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid placing under tree canopy. Signals must be visible from directly above and at angles.

steps.instruction: Add shadow-casting height for visibility from low angles.
steps.helpDetail: Stack rocks, logs, or mound dirt to create 3D signals that cast shadows. Shadows dramatically increase visibility.

techniques.name: Whistle Signal
techniques.subtitle: Three blasts = help needed

steps.instruction: Three short blasts = international distress signal.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything (blasts, flashes, fires) means distress. Two blasts means reply/acknowledgment.

steps.instruction: Repeat every minute until you get a response.
steps.helpDetail: A whistle carries further than shouting and requires far less energy. You can whistle all day; you'll lose your voice in hours.

steps.instruction: If no whistle: bang rocks, sticks, or use a loud clapping sound.
steps.helpDetail: Any rhythmic noise in sets of three carries the distress message. Banging a pot lid is very effective.

techniques.name: SOS in Morse Code
techniques.subtitle: · · · — — — · · · (3 short, 3 long, 3 short)

steps.instruction: SOS = 3 short, 3 long, 3 short. Any medium: light, sound, flag.
steps.helpDetail: Short = quick flash/tap. Long = 3× the duration of short. This is the most universally recognized distress signal.

steps.instruction: With a flashlight: quick-quick-quick, looong-looong-looong, quick-quick-quick.
steps.helpDetail: Aim at aircraft, boats, or distant camps. Repeat continuously. Even a phone screen works.

steps.instruction: Write SOS in sand, snow, or any visible surface.
steps.helpDetail: Make letters as large as possible. Use contrasting materials. SOS is understood in every language worldwide.

techniques.name: Maximizing Phone Battery in Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Get the most from your dying phone

steps.instruction: Turn on airplane mode immediately.
steps.helpDetail: The #1 battery drain is your phone searching for cell signal. In wilderness with no signal, it searches continuously, killing the battery.

steps.instruction: Turn off airplane mode ONLY to attempt calls/texts, then back on.
steps.helpDetail: Climb to high ground, turn off airplane mode, attempt contact, then turn it back on. Each attempt should be deliberate.

steps.instruction: Send texts instead of calling — texts use far less power.
steps.helpDetail: Texts also retry automatically and can get through with weaker signals than voice calls.

steps.instruction: 112 and 911 work on any network and without a SIM.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency calls route through ANY available carrier, not just yours. 112 is the international equivalent of 911.

steps.instruction: Before battery dies: text your GPS coordinates to someone.
steps.helpDetail: Open maps app, copy coordinates, text to contact. Even if the text doesn't send immediately, it will queue and send when signal is found.

techniques.name: Reading & Sharing GPS Coordinates
techniques.subtitle: Your exact location in numbers

steps.instruction: On iPhone: Open Compass app — coordinates shown at bottom.
steps.helpDetail: GPS works WITHOUT cell service or WiFi. It uses satellites directly. Your phone can always determine your position.

steps.instruction: On Android: Open Google Maps, tap the blue dot, coordinates appear.
steps.helpDetail: Or download an offline GPS app before your trip. Offline maps + GPS = full navigation without service.

steps.instruction: Share coordinates as decimal degrees: e.g., 37.7749, -122.4194.
steps.helpDetail: Decimal degrees are the most universal and least error-prone format. Include the negative sign for south/west coordinates.

steps.instruction: What3Words: every 3m×3m square has a unique 3-word address.
steps.helpDetail: The app works offline. Example: ///filled.count.soap. Emergency services in many countries accept What3Words addresses.

techniques.name: Self-Rescue Decision Making
techniques.subtitle: Stay or go? The critical decision.

steps.instruction: Default: STAY where you are. Rescuers search your last known location.
steps.helpDetail: If someone knows your itinerary, staying put is almost always correct. Moving makes you harder to find.

steps.instruction: Go only if: no one knows you're missing, immediate danger, or you know EXACTLY where safety is.
steps.helpDetail: If you self-rescue, leave signs of your direction: arrows in dirt, torn cloth on branches, rock cairns.

steps.instruction: Before leaving, build a visible signal at your current location.
steps.helpDetail: Ground-to-air signal, rock cairn, tied clothing on a branch. Leave a note with your name, date, direction of travel, and condition.

steps.instruction: Travel downhill, follow water downstream — civilization is at lower elevations.
steps.helpDetail: Streams → rivers → towns. Following water also ensures access to drinking water during travel.

techniques.name: Leaving a Trail for Rescuers
techniques.subtitle: Breadcrumbs that save your life

steps.instruction: Build rock cairns (stacked stones) at regular intervals.
steps.helpDetail: 3 rocks stacked is unnatural enough to catch a searcher's eye. Place at decision points (trail junctions, river crossings).

steps.instruction: Break branches at eye level pointing your direction.
steps.helpDetail: Snap a branch so it dangles without falling off. The broken end points your direction of travel.

steps.instruction: Tie bright cloth strips to vegetation.
steps.helpDetail: Cut strips from clothing. Tie at eye level. Use the brightest material available. Space every 100-200 yards.

steps.instruction: Drag a stick or mark the ground in soft terrain.
steps.helpDetail: In mud, sand, or snow — drag a line or make arrows. These are visible from the air and ground.

techniques.name: Helicopter Landing Zone Prep
techniques.subtitle: Clear a safe landing area

steps.instruction: Clear a flat area at least 60×60 feet (20×20 meters).
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose debris, branches, and anything that could blow into the rotor. Mark the perimeter with rocks or logs.

steps.instruction: Remove all overhead obstructions — trees, wires, branches.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopter rotors extend beyond the fuselage. Any overhead contact is catastrophic. Clear completely.

steps.instruction: Mark wind direction with a flag, smoke, or streamers.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters prefer to land into the wind. Hang a cloth strip or light a small smoky fire at the edge of the landing zone.

steps.instruction: Stay upwind and crouch when helicopter approaches.
steps.helpDetail: The downwash is extreme — loose items become projectiles. Shield your eyes. Never approach from the tail rotor side. Wait for pilot signal.

techniques.name: Morse Code SOS
techniques.subtitle: Universal distress: ... --- ...

steps.instruction: SOS: three short, three long, three short.
steps.helpDetail: ... --- ... (dit dit dit / dah dah dah / dit dit dit). Universal distress.

steps.instruction: Signal with flashlight, mirror, whistle, or tapping.
steps.helpDetail: Short = quick flash/tap. Long = 3x duration of short.

steps.instruction: Repeat with 1-minute pauses between sets.
steps.helpDetail: Signal 1 minute, rest 1 minute. Conserve energy.

techniques.name: Helicopter Landing Zone
techniques.subtitle: Clear 100-foot zone for helicopter rescue

steps.instruction: Clear a flat area at least 100x100 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose debris. Rotor wash launches objects at lethal speed.

steps.instruction: Mark with bright material. H = need helicopter. V = need help.
steps.helpDetail: X means cannot move. Use clothing, tarp, or signal panels.

steps.instruction: Indicate wind direction with smoke or flag.
steps.helpDetail: Helicopters land into wind. Light smoky fire upwind of LZ.

steps.instruction: Stand upwind, crouch when approaching. Never approach from rear.
steps.helpDetail: Tail rotor kills. Approach front/side only when pilot signals. Stay low.

techniques.name: Trail Blazing Markers
techniques.subtitle: Leave a trail others can follow

steps.instruction: Break branches at shoulder height pointing your direction.
steps.helpDetail: Pale broken wood visible from distance. Point break in travel direction.

steps.instruction: Stack 3 rocks (cairn) at every turn or decision point.
steps.helpDetail: Three stacked rocks is universal trail marker.

steps.instruction: Scratch arrows on trees or dirt at junctions.
steps.helpDetail: Use knife, sharp rock, or stick. Eye level on trees.

steps.instruction: Tie bright cloth strips to branches every 50-100 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Tear clothing if needed. Visible in both directions.

techniques.name: International Ground Signal Codes
techniques.subtitle: V, X, I — pilot-readable from 1000 feet

steps.instruction: V = Need Help. X = Need Medical. I = Need Supplies.
steps.helpDetail: ICAO standard signals. Make at least 10 feet tall.

steps.instruction: Arrow = traveling this direction. Y = Yes. N = No.
steps.helpDetail: Answer air queries with Y and N on ground.

steps.instruction: Build with rocks, logs, clothing, or trenches.
steps.helpDetail: Maximum contrast with ground color.

steps.instruction: Minimum 10 feet long, 3 feet wide. Bigger is better.
steps.helpDetail: 3-foot letters invisible from 1000 feet. Think highway-scale.

techniques.name: Colored Smoke Signals
techniques.subtitle: Daytime signaling with combustion

steps.instruction: Build a standard fire and get it burning hot.
steps.helpDetail: You need a strong updraft to carry the smoke high.

steps.instruction: add GREEN vegetation for WHITE smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Green leaves, moss, or grass produce thick white smoke. Good against dark backgrounds (trees).

steps.instruction: Add OIL, RUBBER, or PLASTIC for BLACK smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Tires, oil-soaked rags, or plastic produce black smoke. Good against light backgrounds (snow, sand).

steps.instruction: Create 3 distinct puffs (cover/uncover) for SOS.
steps.helpDetail: Use a blanket or coat. Cover the fire, let smoke build, release. Three puffs is the international distress signal.

techniques.name: Personal Locator Beacon (PLB)
techniques.subtitle: One button sends your GPS to rescue coordination

steps.instruction: Deploy the antenna fully and activate the beacon.
steps.helpDetail: Most PLBs have a spring-loaded antenna and a protected activation button. Remove the cover, extend antenna, press and hold for 1 second.

steps.instruction: Place beacon with clear sky view — no canopy, no overhangs.
steps.helpDetail: PLBs transmit on 406 MHz to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites. Signal needs unobstructed path to sky. Even partial canopy degrades signal.

steps.instruction: Leave activated for minimum 24 hours. Do not turn off and on.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple satellite passes improve position accuracy from 5km to 100m. Intermittent activation confuses the system.

steps.instruction: Stay near the beacon. Rescuers will come to the signal location.
steps.helpDetail: If you move, carry the beacon. Response times: 1-4 hours in populated areas, 8-24 hours in remote wilderness.

techniques.name: VHF Radio Distress Call
techniques.subtitle: Channel 16: international distress frequency

steps.instruction: Tune to Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) — monitored by Coast Guard worldwide.
steps.helpDetail: Channel 16 is the international calling and distress frequency. All marine vessels and coast guard stations monitor it 24/7.

steps.instruction: Press transmit and say: MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, this is [your name/vessel].
steps.helpDetail: MAYDAY = life-threatening emergency. PAN-PAN = urgent but not life-threatening. SECURITE = safety information. Use MAYDAY for survival situations.

steps.instruction: State: position, nature of emergency, number of people, assistance needed.
steps.helpDetail: Example: 'Position 37.7749 north, 122.4194 west. Party of 3, one injured. Need helicopter evacuation. Over.'

steps.instruction: Release transmit button and listen for response. Repeat every 2 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: VHF range is line-of-sight: 5-25 miles depending on terrain and antenna height. Move to high ground to extend range.

techniques.name: Night Signaling Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Be visible when darkness hides everything

steps.instruction: Maintain a bright fire on the highest accessible point.
steps.helpDetail: A hilltop fire is visible for 20+ miles on a clear night. Use dry hardwood for sustained bright flames, not smoldering coals.

steps.instruction: Use flashlight or phone flash in SOS pattern.
steps.helpDetail: Three short flashes, three long flashes, three short flashes. Repeat. Even a dim light is visible for miles at night.

steps.instruction: Create a torch from resinous wood or cloth soaked in fat.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark, pine fatwood, or any cloth with animal fat/oil burns brightly. Wave the torch in a figure-8 pattern for attention.

steps.instruction: Use reflective material to catch any light source.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency blankets, foil, even wet metal surfaces reflect moonlight and starlight. Hang them at angles facing likely rescue directions.

techniques.name: Vehicle-Based Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Turn your vehicle into a rescue beacon

steps.instruction: Stay with the vehicle — it is more visible than you are.
steps.helpDetail: A car is visible from aircraft. A person in the wilderness is nearly invisible. Your vehicle is your best signal, shelter, and resource.

steps.instruction: Open hood and trunk — this signals distress to aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: An open hood is a recognized distress signal. Tie bright fabric to the antenna or mirror. Arrange floor mats on the roof as signals.

steps.instruction: Use mirrors, headlights, horn for active signaling.
steps.helpDetail: Side mirrors angle sunlight. Headlights flash at night. Horn blasts carry far. Conserve battery — use in bursts when rescue is likely.

steps.instruction: Burn oil or tires for thick black smoke (extreme emergency only).
steps.helpDetail: Drain oil onto dirt, ignite with battery spark. Black smoke is visible for miles against any sky. Use only when rescue is imminent.

techniques.name: Improvised Flag Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Attract attention with movement and color

steps.instruction: Tie the brightest fabric you have to a long pole or branch.
steps.helpDetail: Orange, red, or yellow are most visible against natural backgrounds. Even a white undershirt works against dark terrain.

steps.instruction: Wave in large, slow arcs — both arms overhead means 'need help.'
steps.helpDetail: Both arms raised and waving is the international ground-to-air distress gesture. One arm raised means 'all okay.'

steps.instruction: Plant the flag on the highest visible point when not actively waving.
steps.helpDetail: Wind keeps it moving, which catches attention. Moving objects are detected 10x faster than static ones by human vision.

techniques.name: Drone Search Awareness
techniques.subtitle: Modern SAR uses drones — know how to be found

steps.instruction: Maximize thermal contrast — drones often use infrared cameras.
steps.helpDetail: Your body heat stands out against cold ground. Clear surroundings of insulating debris. Lie in an open area if you hear a drone.

steps.instruction: Create large, clear ground signals visible from directly above.
steps.helpDetail: Drones fly lower than aircraft but look straight down. Make signals at least 6 feet in any dimension with maximum color contrast.

steps.instruction: Wave reflective materials — drones detect moving objects and reflections.
steps.helpDetail: Emergency blankets, phone screens, anything shiny. Movement triggers drone operator attention faster than static signals.

techniques.name: Satellite Messenger Usage
techniques.subtitle: Two-way emergency communication anywhere on Earth

steps.instruction: Activate SOS function — press and hold the dedicated SOS button.
steps.helpDetail: Garmin inReach, SPOT, Zoleo all have dedicated SOS buttons. This connects to GEOS International Emergency Response Center.

steps.instruction: Send a preset message with your GPS coordinates.
steps.helpDetail: Pre-configure emergency contacts and messages before your trip. Include medical conditions, group size, and planned route.

steps.instruction: Use two-way messaging to communicate situation details.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike PLBs, satellite messengers allow back-and-forth communication. You can describe injuries, conditions, and accessibility for rescue teams.

steps.instruction: Conserve battery — turn off tracking, reduce message frequency.
steps.helpDetail: Most satellite messengers last 24-100 hours. Turn off interval tracking. Send messages only when needed. Keep warm to preserve battery.

techniques.name: Throw Bag River Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Reach a person in moving water

steps.instruction: Position yourself downstream of the person in the water.
steps.helpDetail: The person is being carried toward you. Position yourself on a stable bank where you can brace yourself.

steps.instruction: Throw the bag PAST the person — the rope falls across them.
steps.helpDetail: Aim downstream of the person. Underhanded throw. The bag trails the rope which the person grabs.

steps.instruction: Brace against a solid object — tree, rock, or sitting position.
steps.helpDetail: The force of a person in current is enormous. Without bracing, you will be pulled in. Wrap rope around your hips, not your hands.

steps.instruction: Let the current swing them to shore — do NOT try to pull upstream.
steps.helpDetail: Hold the rope and let the current pendulum the person toward your bank. Fighting the current is impossible.

techniques.name: Cliff and Canyon Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Signal from terrain that blocks normal methods

steps.instruction: Use sound amplification — canyon walls reflect and amplify whistles.
steps.helpDetail: A whistle in a canyon can be heard miles further than in open terrain. Sound bounces between walls creating natural amplification.

steps.instruction: Create signals on the canyon rim if possible, not the floor.
steps.helpDetail: Canyon floors are invisible from the air and from distance. Any signal on the rim is worth 10 signals on the floor.

steps.instruction: Use smoke — it rises out of canyons and is visible from above.
steps.helpDetail: Smoke is the one signal type that works from canyon bottoms. Hot air rises, carrying smoke up and out of the canyon.

techniques.name: Improvised Flotation Device
techniques.subtitle: Stay afloat with what you have

steps.instruction: Pants flotation: tie off leg openings, inflate by scooping air.
steps.helpDetail: Remove pants, tie each leg end in a knot, hold waist open and scoop over your head to trap air. Provides 15-20 minutes of buoyancy.

steps.instruction: Use any sealed container — water bottles, dry bags, sealed coolers.
steps.helpDetail: Two empty 2-liter bottles can support an adult's head above water. Stuff clothing into a garbage bag for a makeshift float.

steps.instruction: Logs, branches, and debris make effective rafts with cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Even a single thick log supports significant weight. Green wood floats well. Lash with rope, belts, vines, or torn fabric strips.

steps.instruction: Survival float: face down, lift head only to breathe.
steps.helpDetail: Also called 'dead man's float.' Arms and legs hang relaxed. Lift face to inhale, then relax. Uses almost zero energy. Can sustain for hours.

techniques.name: Ground-to-Air Signal Panel
techniques.subtitle: Create large visual signals visible from aircraft

steps.instruction: Create a large V (need help), X (need medical), or arrow (traveling this direction).
steps.helpDetail: International ground-to-air signals: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, arrow = traveling this direction, I = need supplies, F = need food/water, Y = yes, N = no.

steps.instruction: Make signals at least 10 feet long using contrasting materials.
steps.helpDetail: Use branches, rocks, clothing, or dig trenches in soil. CONTRAST is key: dark materials on light ground, light materials on dark ground. Shadows from trenches or piled rocks are visible from high altitude.

steps.instruction: Place in open areas — meadows, beaches, ridgetops. Away from tree canopy.
steps.helpDetail: Aircraft cannot see through tree canopy. Place signals in the largest open area available. Multiple signals increase detection probability. Maintain signals daily — wind and weather degrade them.

techniques.name: Survival Whistle Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Use standardized whistle patterns for rescue communication

steps.instruction: Three blasts = universal distress signal. Pause. Repeat.
steps.helpDetail: Three of anything is the international distress signal: 3 whistle blasts, 3 fires, 3 gunshots. Blow three sharp blasts, pause 1 minute, repeat. A whistle carries 1-2 miles — much farther than shouting.

steps.instruction: Two blasts = 'come to me' or acknowledgment from rescuers.
steps.helpDetail: If you hear 2 blasts in response to your 3, rescuers have heard you and are coming. Continue blowing 3 blasts periodically so they can home in on your position.

steps.instruction: One long blast = 'attention' or 'where are you?'
steps.helpDetail: Used to get attention or locate group members. A whistle is the single most important signaling device to carry. It works when you're too exhausted to shout. It works in fog, darkness, and wind.

techniques.name: Water Rescue Throw Line
techniques.subtitle: Rescue someone from water without entering it yourself

steps.instruction: Throw a rope, cord, or improvised line to the person in the water.
steps.helpDetail: Rule: REACH, THROW, ROW, GO (in that order of safety). Try to reach with a stick first. Then throw a line. Only enter water as absolute last resort — drowning victims pull rescuers under.

steps.instruction: Attach a flotation device to the line if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Tie a water bottle, sealed container, or life jacket to the end. This gives them something to grab and provides buoyancy. Throw PAST the victim and pull line across them.

steps.instruction: Brace yourself firmly and pull them in slowly.
steps.helpDetail: Sit down, brace feet against rocks or trees. A panicking victim pulls with surprising force. Pull steadily — don't let them pull you in. Talk calmly and firmly: 'Hold on. I'm pulling you in.'

techniques.name: Vehicle Emergency Signaling
techniques.subtitle: Use a disabled vehicle as a rescue beacon

steps.instruction: Stay with the vehicle — it's much easier to spot than a person.
steps.helpDetail: A car is visible from aircraft at 10+ miles. A person on foot is visible from less than 1 mile. Your vehicle provides shelter, signaling tools, and materials. The #1 cause of vehicle-stranding deaths is leaving the vehicle.

steps.instruction: Use mirrors, hood, CDs, or any reflective surface to signal aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: Side mirrors can be removed for hand-signaling. Open the hood — it's an unusual sight that attracts attention. Hang bright clothing on the antenna. Lay out floor mats in an X pattern nearby.

steps.instruction: If safe, burn the spare tire for black smoke signal (extreme last resort).
steps.helpDetail: Tire fires produce thick black smoke visible for 20+ miles. Only burn if rescue is actively being sought and you need rapid attention. Keep fire away from the fuel tank. Flash headlights at night.

techniques.name: Cold Water Self-Rescue
techniques.subtitle: Survive cold water immersion using the 1-10-1 principle

steps.instruction: 1 MINUTE: control your breathing. The cold shock response lasts ~1 minute.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water triggers gasping and hyperventilation. This is the most dangerous phase — most cold water drownings happen in the first minute. Focus ALL effort on controlled breathing. Don't try to swim yet.

steps.instruction: 10 MINUTES: you have ~10 minutes of meaningful movement.
steps.helpDetail: Cold incapacitates muscles progressively. You have roughly 10 minutes to self-rescue before your arms and legs stop working. Swim to safety NOW if close. If not, prepare to float.

steps.instruction: 1 HOUR: hypothermia sets in after ~1 hour in cold water. Assume HELP position.
steps.helpDetail: HELP position: Heat Escape Lessening Position. Cross arms over chest, pull knees to chest. This reduces heat loss from armpits and groin. Stay as still as possible — movement accelerates heat loss.

techniques.name: Helicopter Rescue Procedures
techniques.subtitle: Safely interact with rescue helicopters

steps.instruction: Clear a landing zone: 100x100 feet minimum, flat, no loose debris.
steps.helpDetail: Remove loose branches, clothing, tarps — rotor wash sends everything flying at high speed. Mark wind direction with smoke or a flag. Helicopter lands INTO the wind. Mark the zone with bright items at corners.

steps.instruction: Signal the helicopter: both arms raised in a Y = need help. One arm up = all OK.
steps.helpDetail: Y (both arms raised) = Yes, I need help. One arm raised, one down = all OK / negative. Waving one arm side to side = distress. Do NOT wave both arms wildly — it can mean 'don't land.'

steps.instruction: Approach from the FRONT and DOWNHILL side. NEVER approach from behind (tail rotor).
steps.helpDetail: The tail rotor is nearly invisible and instantly lethal. Always approach where the pilot can see you. Stay low — rotor blades dip. On slopes, approach from the downhill side. Wait for crew signal before approaching.

techniques.name: Smoke Signal Timing & Technique
techniques.subtitle: Maximize smoke signal effectiveness for aerial detection

steps.instruction: Build three fires in a triangle — three smoke columns = distress signal.
steps.helpDetail: Space fires 100-200 feet apart in an equilateral triangle. Three columns of smoke is the international distress signal. One column of smoke looks like a campfire. Three is unmistakably a call for help.

steps.instruction: Use green vegetation for white smoke on dark backgrounds, tires/oil for dark smoke on light backgrounds.
steps.helpDetail: Match smoke color to background for contrast. Against dark forest: white smoke (green branches, moss, wet leaves). Against light sky/snow: dark smoke (rubber, oil, plastic). Contrast = visibility.

steps.instruction: Keep signal fires prepared but unlit. Light when you see or hear aircraft.
steps.helpDetail: Pre-build signal fires with tinder and kindling ready. Put a cover over them to keep dry. When you detect aircraft, light all three quickly. Keep green branches nearby to throw on for smoke.

techniques.name: Signal Kite Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a kite to increase visual presence above tree canopy

steps.instruction: Build a diamond kite frame from two crossed sticks.
steps.helpDetail: Vertical stick: 36 inches. Horizontal stick: 24 inches. Cross them at 1/3 from the top of the vertical stick. Lash the intersection tightly. Notch the ends of each stick.

steps.instruction: Cover with plastic bag, poncho, or large leaves tied to the frame.
steps.helpDetail: String cord through the notches to create the outline. Stretch plastic or fabric over the frame and secure. Use the brightest colored material available. A tail of cloth strips helps stability.

steps.instruction: Attach a long line and fly in open area. Tie bright streamers to the line.
steps.helpDetail: A kite flying 100+ feet above tree canopy is visible for miles. Attach reflective tape, bright fabric, or foil pieces to the line. Even in moderate wind, a properly built kite extends your visual signature dramatically.

techniques.name: Urban Search & Rescue Marking
techniques.subtitle: Use FEMA X-marking to communicate with rescue teams

steps.instruction: Draw a large X on the outside of the building entrance.
steps.helpDetail: This is the FEMA Disaster Marking System. The X divides into 4 quadrants. Use spray paint, chalk, or marker. Make it large enough to see from the street.

steps.instruction: Fill quadrants: top = team ID, left = date/time, right = hazards, bottom = victims found.
steps.helpDetail: Top: your identifier. Left: date and time of search. Right: hazards present (rats, gas leak, structural damage). Bottom: number of live/dead victims (e.g., '2L/1D' = 2 living, 1 dead).

steps.instruction: A single slash means search in progress. Complete X means search finished.
steps.helpDetail: When you begin searching: draw one slash (\). When complete: add the second slash to complete the X and fill in quadrants. This prevents duplicate searches and communicates status to other teams.

techniques.name: River Self-Rescue Position
techniques.subtitle: Survive being swept away in river current

steps.instruction: Roll onto your BACK, feet pointed DOWNSTREAM.
steps.helpDetail: Your feet act as bumpers against rocks and obstacles. If you go headfirst, you risk fatal head and spinal injuries. Keep your feet up near the surface to avoid foot entrapment in rocks on the bottom.

steps.instruction: Use your arms to steer toward the bank. Angle ferry across the current.
steps.helpDetail: Don't fight the current directly — angle across it at 45 degrees toward shore. Use backstroke to create lateral movement. Current will carry you downstream while you gradually move toward the bank.

steps.instruction: If approaching a strainer (downed tree), flip to FRONT and swim hard toward it.
steps.helpDetail: Strainers (fallen trees, debris) are the #1 killer in rivers. They trap and drown. If you can't avoid one, face it head-on, swim aggressively, and try to climb OVER it. Never go under or sideways.

techniques.name: Self-Evacuation Decision Making
techniques.subtitle: Decide when to stay put vs. self-rescue

steps.instruction: Stay put IF: someone knows your location, you filed a trip plan, you're injured, or terrain is dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: Search and rescue finds stationary targets much faster than moving ones. If anyone knows where you are, staying put is almost always the better choice. You're easier to find, you conserve energy, and you reduce injury risk.

steps.instruction: Self-evacuate IF: no one knows you're missing, you know your location, and you're uninjured.
steps.helpDetail: If nobody will report you missing, nobody will search for you. In this case, self-rescue is your only option. You must know approximately where you are and which direction leads to help.

steps.instruction: Leave clear signs at your camp indicating direction of travel.
steps.helpDetail: If you leave camp: leave a note (under a rock, protected from weather), build arrows pointing your direction, mark trees along your route. This lets rescuers follow you if a search begins after you leave.

techniques.name: Improvised Litter Carry Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Transport an injured person without commercial equipment

steps.instruction: Build a pole litter: two poles + fabric/jacket threaded between them.
steps.helpDetail: Two sturdy poles 7-8 feet long. Thread jackets (zipped closed, arms inside) onto the poles. Or use a blanket/tarp folded around the poles. Test with your own weight before loading the patient.

steps.instruction: For one-person carry: use a backpack frame as a seat carry.
steps.helpDetail: Patient sits on a large backpack carried on your back. Their legs hang over your hips. Secure with straps. This works for conscious patients with lower extremity injuries.

steps.instruction: Carry team rotation: switch every 200 meters to prevent exhaustion.
steps.helpDetail: A 150-lb patient on a litter is exhausting. Rotate carriers every 200 meters or every 5 minutes. Need minimum 4 carriers (2 working, 2 resting). Over rough terrain, 6-8 carriers is ideal.

articles.title: How Search & Rescue Works
articles.body:
Understanding SAR operations helps you cooperate with rescuers.

Timeline:
- Someone reports you missing → SAR activation (2-24 hours)
- Initial search: last known position, planned route, nearest trails (Day 1)
- Expanded search: wider area based on travel estimates (Days 2-3)
- Extended search: aircraft, dogs, thermal imaging (Days 3-7)
- After 7 days: search intensity decreases significantly

What helps rescuers:
- Filing a trip plan with someone (exact dates, route, vehicle location)
- Carrying a whistle and mirror
- Staying on planned route
- Staying PUT when lost
- Bright clothing (avoid earth tones in wilderness — wear orange, red, yellow)

Personal Locator Beacons (PLBs) and satellite communicators (like Garmin inReach) send your GPS position to rescue coordination centers worldwide. They work anywhere on Earth with clear sky view. They are the single most effective survival tool you can carry.

articles.title: Visual Signaling Techniques
articles.body:
Signal detection depends on contrast and movement.

Contrast:
- Dark signals on light backgrounds (rocks on snow/sand)
- Light signals on dark backgrounds (clothing on dark rock/forest)
- Flame at night, smoke by day
- Reflective flash in sunlight

Movement:
- Moving flags catch attention better than static signals
- Waving both arms overhead is the international distress gesture
- Standing waving is visible further than ground signals at low angles

Signal priorities (most to least effective):
1. PLB/satellite communicator (100% detection)
2. Signal mirror (50+ mile range in sunlight)
3. Signal fire/smoke (10-50 mile range)
4. Ground-to-air symbols (1-5 mile range)
5. Whistle (1/2 mile range in calm conditions)
6. Voice (100-200 yards)

Always use multiple methods simultaneously. An aircraft might miss your ground signal but see your mirror flash.

articles.title: Using Your Phone for Rescue
articles.body:
Your smartphone is a powerful survival tool if used wisely.

Battery conservation:
- Airplane mode extends battery 10-50×
- Reduce screen brightness to minimum
- Close all apps
- Turn off Bluetooth, WiFi, location services when not needed
- Cold batteries drain faster — keep phone warm inside clothing

Emergency calls:
- 911/112 route through ANY carrier (not just yours)
- Work without a SIM card
- Emergency SOS via satellite (iPhone 14+, newer Android devices)
- Google Pixel: Emergency SOS calls and crash detection

Offline capability:
- GPS works WITHOUT service (airplane mode + GPS = works)
- Download offline maps before trips (Google Maps, AllTrails)
- Compass app works offline
- Camera for documenting route, injuries, wildlife

Last resort: If battery is critical, write a text message with your coordinates, name, situation. Hit send. Even if it doesn't send now, it will queue and send when signal is eventually found.

articles.title: Personal Locator Beacons: The Ultimate Safety Net
articles.body:
A PLB is the single most effective rescue tool ever invented for wilderness use.

How PLBs work:
- Transmit on 406 MHz to COSPAS-SARSAT satellites orbiting Earth
- Signal includes unique ID registered to you (name, emergency contacts, medical info)
- Satellites relay your position to the nearest Rescue Coordination Center
- Position accuracy: 100 meters with GPS-equipped PLBs
- Battery life: 24-48 hours continuous transmission

PLB vs. Satellite Messenger vs. EPIRB:
- PLB: one-way SOS, no subscription, 5-year battery, for land use
- Satellite messenger (inReach, SPOT): two-way messaging, subscription required, rechargeable
- EPIRB: maritime version, auto-activates in water, required on commercial vessels

Registration is critical:
- Register at beacons.noaa.gov (US) or your country's equivalent
- Include medical conditions, emergency contacts, typical activities
- Update annually and whenever your information changes
- Unregistered PLBs still trigger rescue but delay response

False activation: If you accidentally activate, do NOT turn it off. Call 1-800-406-8840 (US) to cancel. Turning it off and on creates multiple search targets.

articles.title: Emergency Radio Communication
articles.body:
Radio communication can reach rescuers far beyond visual or auditory signaling range.

Key frequencies:
- VHF Channel 16 (156.800 MHz): International maritime distress
- 121.5 MHz: Aviation emergency (monitored by all aircraft)
- FRS Channel 1 (462.5625 MHz): Common family radio default
- CB Channel 9 (27.065 MHz): Emergency channel for CB radios
- HAM 146.520 MHz: National simplex calling frequency

MAYDAY format (memorize):
1. MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY
2. This is [name/callsign] (repeat 3 times)
3. MAYDAY [name/callsign]
4. My position is [coordinates or description]
5. Nature of distress: [injury, lost, stranded]
6. Number of persons: [count]
7. Assistance required: [evacuation, medical, supplies]
8. Any other useful information
9. OVER

Radio discipline:
- Keep transmissions short (under 30 seconds)
- Say 'OVER' when done transmitting
- Say 'OUT' when conversation is finished
- Listen before transmitting to avoid blocking other calls

articles.title: Water Rescue Principles
articles.body:
Water rescue follows a strict priority hierarchy: Reach, Throw, Row, Go.

Reach: Extend a branch, pole, paddle, or clothing from shore. Safest option.

Throw: Toss a rope, throw bag, life ring, or any floating object. Second safest.

Row: Use a boat, kayak, surfboard, or improvised raft to reach the person. Moderate risk.

Go: Swim to the person. LAST RESORT. More rescuers drown than victims in untrained swim rescues.

If you must enter water:
- Take a flotation device with you
- Approach from behind (panicking people will push you under)
- Tow by collar or hair, keeping distance from flailing arms
- If grabbed, go underwater — drowning people release and swim up toward air

Cold water considerations:
- 50°F water: 1-3 hours survival time
- 40°F water: 30-90 minutes
- 32°F water: 15-45 minutes
- Cold shock response peaks in first 3 minutes — focus on breathing control
- Swim failure occurs at 3-30 minutes — muscles stop responding

articles.title: Being Found at Night
articles.body:
Night creates unique challenges and opportunities for rescue.

Advantages of night:
- Light sources visible for extreme distances (20+ miles)
- Fire visible from aircraft at 50+ miles altitude
- Flashlight visible for 5-10 miles
- Phone screen visible for 1-2 miles
- Less visual clutter — any light stands out

Disadvantages of night:
- Ground signals invisible
- Color-based signals useless
- Rescue teams may suspend ground searches
- Navigation for self-rescue is dangerous

Night signaling strategy:
1. Maintain the brightest fire possible on high ground
2. Flash SOS continuously with any available light
3. Use reflective materials to catch searchlights
4. Sound signals carry further at night (cooler air is denser)
5. Keep whistle and mirror within arm's reach at all times

If you see search lights or hear helicopters:
- Wave a flashlight or burning stick rapidly
- Blow whistle in bursts of three
- Do NOT run toward the search team — you may injure yourself in darkness. Let them come to you.

articles.title: The Science of Survival Signaling
articles.body:
Effective signaling is the difference between days and weeks of waiting for rescue.

Visibility ranges by method:
- Signal mirror: 50+ miles (brightest non-electronic signal)
- Smoke column: 15-25 miles (calm conditions)
- Signal fire at night: 20+ miles from aircraft
- Ground signal panel: 3-5 miles from aircraft
- Flashlight: 5-10 miles at night
- Whistle: 1-2 miles
- Shouting: 1/4 mile at best

The signal mirror is the single most effective signaling tool. On a clear day, a mirror flash can be seen by aircraft over 50 miles away. Practice aiming before you need it.

Signaling principles:
1. Contrast: your signal must look different from the environment
2. Movement: moving signals catch attention better than static ones
3. Pattern: three of anything = distress (3 fires, 3 blasts, 3 flashes)
4. Duration: signal continuously — aircraft pass overhead in seconds
5. Location: high ground, open areas, away from shadows

Common mistake: people signal for 5 minutes then stop. Aircraft may be 30 minutes away. Signal continuously whenever you hear or see potential rescue.

articles.title: Water Rescue: Core Principles
articles.body:
Water is the most dangerous rescue environment. More rescuers die in water rescues than any other type.

The rescue priority order (RETHROG):
1. REACH: extend a stick, paddle, branch from shore
2. THROW: throw a rope, flotation device, anything buoyant
3. ROW: use a boat, raft, or floating platform
4. GO: swim to the victim (LAST RESORT ONLY)

Why 'GO' is last:
- Drowning victims grab anything — including you
- Cold water saps your strength faster than expected
- Current is stronger than you think
- You become a second victim, doubling the rescue problem

If you must enter water:
- Bring a flotation device between you and the victim
- Approach from behind to avoid being grabbed
- If grabbed: dive deep — victims release and try to surface
- Use a cross-chest carry to tow to shore

Swiftwater rules:
- Never stand in water above your knees in current
- Foot entrapment kills — shuffle, don't step
- If swept away: feet downstream, ride it out to calm water

articles.title: Self-Rescue Navigation Strategy
articles.body:
If you decide to self-evacuate, navigation strategy is critical.

Follow water downstream:
- Streams lead to rivers. Rivers lead to settlements.
- Water provides hydration along the route.
- Terrain is generally easier along waterways.
- Exception: canyon country — rivers can lead into impassable gorges.

Follow power lines or roads:
- Even abandoned roads lead somewhere.
- Walk on utility right-of-ways — they're cleared paths.
- Power lines lead to substations, which are near towns.

Travel strategy:
- Walk during cool hours (dawn, dusk) in hot climates
- Walk during warm hours (midday) in cold climates
- Rest when visibility is poor (fog, dark)
- Travel 50 minutes, rest 10 minutes
- Mark your trail so you don't circle

Pacing:
- Over flat terrain: expect 2-3 miles per hour
- Over rough terrain: expect 1 mile per hour
- Mountainous: expect 1/2 mile per hour
- Add 1 hour per 1,000 feet elevation gain

Stopping criteria: if you reach a road, trail, river, or fence line, follow it. These all lead to civilization.

articles.title: Group Rescue Coordination
articles.body:
Groups have advantages in rescue situations but require coordination.

Assign roles immediately:
- Signal chief: maintains signal fires and panels
- Medical lead: monitors injured and sick
- Resource lead: manages food, water, firewood
- Lookout: watches and listens for rescue

Group signaling advantages:
- More materials for building signals
- Can maintain 24-hour signal watch
- Louder audio signals
- More hands for ground-to-air panels
- Can send scouts while maintaining base camp

Group decision-making:
- Decisions by consensus when possible, leader decides when deadlocked
- NEVER split the group unless sending scouts on a clear mission
- Scouts travel in pairs, carry signal devices, and have a return time
- Missing scouts trigger search protocol, not more scouts

Group morale:
- Maintain schedule and routine
- Assign meaningful tasks to everyone
- Share information openly (hiding bad news erodes trust)
- Celebrate small wins (fire started, water found, signal built)
- Keep idle time busy (improve shelter, build more signals)

articles.title: Search & Rescue: How Rescuers Think
articles.body:
Understanding how SAR teams operate helps you get found faster.

Search activation timeline:
- Overdue report: 4-24 hours after expected return
- SAR alert: 2-6 hours after report
- Field teams deployed: 4-12 hours after alert
- Air assets: daylight hours only (usually)

Search patterns:
- SAR starts at your last known position (LKP)
- They search the most likely route first
- Then expand in concentric circles
- Statistical: 95% of lost persons found within 3 miles of LKP

What helps SAR find you:
1. File a trip plan with route, expected return, and alternative plans
2. Stay on or near your planned route
3. Stay put once lost (moving makes search area exponentially larger)
4. Make yourself visible: clear area, signals, contrast
5. Signal continuously during likely search hours

What hinders SAR:
- No trip plan = no one reports you missing
- Deviating from planned route = searching wrong area
- Moving after being lost = expanding search area
- Camouflaged shelter = invisible from above

The single most important thing: tell someone where you're going and when you'll be back.
